topic: Someone finds an unsigned birthday card in their mailbox
raw_output:
[story 1]

The mail slot clattered as I yanked the heavy box onto the porch. My jaw was tight, a hard knot lodged beneath my skin. I was already running late, the clock mocking me with its relentless tick. Then I saw it, tucked beneath a flyer for a local bake sale: a thick, cream-colored card, utterly blank save for a single, smudged ink mark near the corner. A flash of pure irritation shot through me. Who leaves something like this? It felt like a deliberate slight, a tiny, pointless jab in the otherwise frustrating monotony of my day. I picked it up, the paper feeling flimsy and insulting in my fingers, and immediately crumpled it, shoving it deep into my pocket as if physically erasing the intrusion.

[story 2]

I stared at the pile of junk mail accumulating on the entryway table, my knuckles white where I gripped the edge of the wood. Every piece of paper seemed designed to provoke a reaction, and this particular envelope held the spark. An unsigned birthday card. The sheer audacity of it made my stomach clench. I could feel the heat rising in my neck, a prickly sensation that made my breathing shallow. I didn't want to look. I didn't want to acknowledge the presence of something so carelessly offered. With a sharp exhale that sounded more like a hiss, I swept the card off the table and tossed it toward the recycling bin with unnecessary force. It landed with a soft, defeated thud, and I immediately turned my back, focusing solely on the urgent task at hand, trying to smother the churning in my chest.

[story 3]

My shoulders were hiked up near my ears, a rigid tension holding my posture unnaturally straight. I had been expecting a bill, nothing more, but the sight of that card—a pristine, unopened greeting—sent a jolt of profound displeasure through me. It was so casual, so devoid of context, and it grated on my nerves like sandpaper. I snatched it up, my fingers gripping the card so tightly the edges crinkled. I turned it over, searching for some hidden meaning, some justification for its existence. Finding none, I let out a short, sharp laugh that held no humor whatsoever. I didn't bother to put it in the trash; instead, I folded it precisely, deliberately, and slipped it into the bottom of my briefcase, a silent declaration that whatever this was, it was unwelcome.
